1: wayyiśśāʾ yaʿăqōḇ raglāyw — "Jacob lifted his feet." The MKT renders this "continued on his journey," but the literal is more vivid: nāśāʾ raglāyw (to lift one's feet) is an idiom for setting out with energy and purpose — the feet are lifted rather than dragged. The phrase captures Jacob's renewed momentum after the Bethel theophany (Genesis 28:10-22); he departs with the covenant promise fresh in his ears. The expression appears only here and in Ezekiel 1:7 (for the living creatures' straight legs) — it is unusual enough to be marked as a deliberate stylistic choice by the narrator.
2: wayyarʾ wĕhinnēh bĕʾēr baśśāḏeh — "And he saw — and behold — a well in the field." The narrative well-meeting is a recognized type-scene in biblical literature: a young man travels to a foreign land, meets a young woman at a well, a betrothal follows (cf. Abraham's servant and Rebekah, Genesis 24:11-27; Moses and Zipporah, Exodus 2:15-21). The exclamatory hinnēh (behold!) marks the moment of recognition — the well is the narrative signal that a betrothal-meeting is about to occur. The large stone (ʾeḇen gĕḏōlāh) over the well's mouth is the obstacle that will display Jacob's strength and eagerness in v10.
3: wĕnĕʾespû-šāmmāh kol-hāʿĕḏārîm wĕgālălû ʾet-hāʾeḇen — "When all the flocks were gathered, they would roll the stone." The communal requirement — all flocks assembled before the stone is moved — establishes a social norm that Jacob will violate in v10 (rolling the stone alone, before all flocks arrive). The Qal perfect verbs here describe habitual action (wĕnĕʾespû... wĕgālălû): what was customarily done. The stone's communal guarding of the water reflects a water-rights culture in which shared wells had strict rules of access — one group could not monopolize the water at the expense of others.
4: ʾaḥay mēʾayin ʾattem — "My brothers, where are you from?" Jacob's address ʾaḥay (my brothers) is the conventional greeting to strangers of one's approximate social standing in the biblical world — not claiming literal kinship but establishing social solidarity. The inquiry about origin (mēʾayin ʾattem) is the standard opening of a self-identification dialogue — the same pattern in Ruth 2:5 (Boaz's inquiry about Ruth) and 1 Samuel 30:13 (David's inquiry of the Egyptian servant). The dialogue format (question / answer / question / answer) follows the convention of biblical encounter narratives.
5: hăyĕḏaʿtem ʾet-lāḇān ben-nāḥôr — "Do you know Laban, son of Nahor?" Jacob identifies Laban by grandfather rather than by father (Bethuel) — possibly because Nahor was more well-known as the founder of the clan, or because Bethuel's status in the narrative was reduced (he appears only briefly in Genesis 24:50). The verb yāḏaʿ (to know) in this inquiry is social knowledge — do you recognize/know who he is? The affirmative answer (yāḏāʿnû, "we know him") uses the same verb, confirming that they have direct acquaintance with him.
6: hăšālôm lô — "Is he well?" The idiom šālôm lô (is it peace/wholeness to him?) is the standard Hebrew greeting inquiry — not a casual "how is he" but a covenantal inquiry about the total wellbeing of a person. Šālôm (H7965) encompasses physical health, social harmony, relational wholeness. The shepherds' answer (šālôm — "peace/well") completes the formulaic exchange. The additional information — "here comes his daughter Rachel with the sheep" — arrives as confirmation while Jacob is still processing the positive answer; the narrative timing creates suspense.
7: lōʾ-ʿēt hēʾāsep hammiqneh — "It is not time for the livestock to be gathered." Jacob's instruction to the shepherds (water the sheep and take them back to pasture) is either a desire for privacy with Rachel, or a reflection of his knowledge of pastoral customs that the shepherds are flouting. The observation about the sun still being high (gāḏôl hayyôm, the day is great/big) uses the same adjective (gāḏôl) as the greatness of the stone in vv2-3 — the great stone, the great day — both obstacles to the encounter Jacob wants.
8: lōʾ nûḵal ʿaḏ ʾăšer yēʾāsĕpû kol-hāʿĕḏārîm — "We cannot, until all the flocks are gathered." The shepherds' response appeals to the communal water-rights custom of v3 — the stone cannot be moved by less than the full assembly. The social constraint creates dramatic irony: Jacob is about to violate the very rule they cite by rolling the stone alone. Their "we cannot" (lōʾ nûḵal) is answered by Jacob's unilateral action in v10 — what the community could not do alone, Jacob does for love.
9: wĕrāḥēl bāʾāh ʿim-haṣṣōʾn ʾăšer lĕʾāḇîhā kî rōʿāh hîʾ — "Rachel came with her father's flock, for she was their shepherdess." The word rōʿāh (shepherdess, Qal feminine participle of rāʿāh) is unusual — women shepherds appear in biblical narrative at significant moments (Moses's future wife Zipporah is a shepherdess in Exodus 2:16-17; the Song of Solomon's beloved is associated with pastoral imagery). The feminine participle here marks Rachel's role as active keeper of the flock — she is tending, not merely accompanying. The temporal phrase "while he was still talking with them" compresses the timing so that Rachel's arrival is simultaneous with the failed instruction to the shepherds.
10: wayyāgar yaʿăqōḇ wayyāgel ʾet-hāʾeḇen — "Jacob went over and rolled the stone." The verse breaks the communal rule established in vv3 and 8: Jacob rolls the stone alone, immediately, without waiting for all flocks to gather. The verb gālal (H1556, to roll, to roll away) will recur at the naming of Galeed (Genesis 31:47-48) and Galilee's association with rolling/revealing. The physical act of single-handedly moving what normally required the assembled community is a display of extraordinary strength driven by emotional urgency. Notably, Jacob does this for his uncle's sheep, not his own — the act is an unrequested gift of labor, foreshadowing the fourteen years of service he will give to Laban.
11: wayyiššaq yaʿăqōḇ lĕrāḥēl wayyiśśāʾ ʾet-qōlô wayyēḇk — "Jacob kissed Rachel and raised his voice and wept." The kiss (nāšaq, H5401, to kiss; also used for battle-joining and equipment-touching — a contact verb) and the weeping are simultaneous. Wayyiśśāʾ ʾet-qōlô — "he raised his voice" — is the standard idiom for crying aloud (cf. 21:16 for Hagar's weeping; 27:38 for Esau's cry). Jacob's tears are explained in v12 — it is the recognition of finding his family after the flight from home. The emotional rawness of the moment is preserved without editorializing — the narrator does not explain the tears; the reader fills in the isolation, fear, and relief that generated them.
12: wayyaggēḏ lĕrāḥēl kî ʾăḥî ʾāḇîhā hûʾ wĕḵî ben-riqāh hûʾ — "He told Rachel that he was a kinsman of her father and a son of Rebekah." The word ʾāḥ (brother/kinsman, H251) is used broadly in Hebrew for both siblings and extended kin — Jacob is actually a nephew of Laban, not a brother. The term ʾăḥî ʾāḇîhā ("kinsman of her father") is the narrator's gloss of what Jacob said; the fuller kinship identifier "son of Rebekah" (Laban's sister) establishes the genealogical connection that will ground the subsequent labor-covenant.
13: wayyārāṣ liqrāʾtô wayyĕḥabbēq-lô wayyĕnaššeq-lô — "He ran to meet him, and embraced him, and kissed him." Laban's greeting uses the same three verbs — run (rûṣ), embrace (ḥābaq), kiss (nāšaq) — that will appear in the father's reception of the prodigal son in Luke 15:20 (and in Abraham's servant's reception in Genesis 24:29-31, another Laban episode). The warm reception may reflect genuine hospitality or, as the narrative will reveal, Laban's recognition of a useful kinsman. Wayyĕsappēr lĕlāḇān ʾēt kol-haddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh — "Jacob told him all these things" — the contents of the telling are not specified; the reader is left to imagine the account of the deception, flight, and Bethel theophany that constitute Jacob's recent history.
14: ʾaḵ ʿaṣmî ûḇĕśārî ʾattāh — "Surely you are my bone and my flesh." Laban's kinship declaration uses the same formula Adam used for Eve in Genesis 2:23 (ʿeṣem mēʿăṣāmay ûḇāśār mibĕśārî, "bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh") — the formula of belonging-by-substance. In 2:23 the formula establishes marriage; here it establishes covenant kinship. The words will acquire ironic weight in the subsequent narrative: the one who claims Jacob as bone and flesh will exploit him for twenty years. Ḥōḏeš yāmîm — "a month of days" — the Hebrew idiom for a full month; the time-reckoning before the labor-covenant is proposed.
15: kî-ʾāḥî ʾattāh waʿăḇaḏtanî ḥinnām haggîḏāh-lî mah-maškoroteyḵā — "Just because you are my kinsman, should you work for me for nothing? Tell me what your wages should be." Laban's framing is ostensibly generous: kinship should not mean free labor. But the question shifts the relationship from household-kinship (where Jacob might legitimately expect hospitality) to labor-contract. The word māśkōret (H4909, wages, remuneration from śāḵar = to hire) introduces the commercial register that will dominate the Jacob-Laban relationship. Laban's opening move to set wages — when Jacob has not asked for pay — reveals his calculative nature: he wants to fix the terms before Jacob's expectations grow.
16: ûlĕlāḇān šĕtê ḇānôt šēm hāgĕḏōlāh lēʾāh wĕšēm haqqĕṭannāh rāḥēl — "Laban had two daughters; the name of the elder was Leah, and the name of the younger was Rachel." The narrator introduces both daughters at this point to set up the deception of v23. The age-ordering — elder (gĕḏōlāh) Leah, younger (qĕṭannāh) Rachel — echoes the elder-younger tension that runs throughout Genesis: Cain/Abel, Ishmael/Isaac, Esau/Jacob. The names: Lēʾāh may derive from an Akkadian cognate for "wild cow" or a Hebrew word for "weary"; Rāḥēl = "ewe" (the young female sheep) — both names are pastoral, fitting the shepherd family.
17: wĕʿênê lēʾāh rakkôt wĕrāḥēl hāyĕtāh yĕpat-tōʾar wîpat marʾeh — "Leah's eyes were soft/weak, but Rachel was beautiful in form and beautiful in appearance." The description of Leah's eyes is the most-debated phrase in this chapter: rakkôt (H7390, tender, soft, weak) applied to eyes can mean: (a) tender/lovely — eyes with a gentle, pleasing quality (a compliment in some ANE traditions); (b) weak/dull — eyes lacking the luster that ancient beauty standards prized. Most modern translators choose the negative reading because it contrasts with Rachel's beauty. The double beauty formula for Rachel — yĕpat-tōʾar (beautiful of form/shape) and yĕpat marʾeh (beautiful of appearance/face) — is the superlative double used elsewhere only for Joseph (Genesis 39:6, yĕpeh-tōʾar wîpeh marʾeh) and Esther (Genesis 2:7). Rachel and Joseph share the same beauty formula, another connection between mother and son.
18: wayyeʾĕhaḇ yaʿăqōḇ ʾet-rāḥēl wayyōʾmer ʾeʿĕḇāḏĕḵā šeḇaʿ šānîm bĕrāḥēl bittĕḵā haqqĕṭannāh — "Jacob loved Rachel and said, 'I will serve you seven years for Rachel your younger daughter.'" The verb ʾāhaḇ (to love) is stated before any marriage discussion — love precedes and grounds the labor-contract. Jacob's offer is extraordinarily high: the standard bride-price (môhar) was thirty shekels of silver (Deuteronomy 22:29); seven years of labor at a typical daily wage was worth far more. The specificity "your younger daughter" (bittĕḵā haqqĕṭannāh) is Jacob's legally precise identification — naming Rachel by birth-order specifically so there is no ambiguity. Laban's subsequent deception will exploit exactly this contractual specificity.
19: ṭôḇ tittî ʾōtāh lĕḵā mimmittî ʾōtāh lĕʾîš ʾaḥēr šĕḇāh ʿimmādî — "It is better that I give her to you than to another man. Stay with me." Laban's acceptance is conditional only that Jacob stay — he does not explicitly promise to give Rachel, a legally significant evasion. He says tittî ʾōtāh lĕḵā (I will give her to you) — apparently confirming the covenant — but the lack of explicit naming of which daughter will later be exploited. The parallel between Laban's deception of Jacob and Jacob's deception of Isaac is structural: both involve a covered identity, a darkened or confused setting, and the wrong person receiving what was promised to another.
20: wayyihyû bĕʿênāyw kĕyāmîm ʾăḥāḏîm bĕʾahaḇātô ʾōtāh — "They were in his eyes like a few days, in his love for her." The phrase kĕyāmîm ʾăḥāḏîm (like a few days, like individual days) is psychologically acute: prolonged time subjectively compressed by intense love. The preposition bĕʾahaḇātô (in his loving of her) — the Qal infinitive construct — makes the love the instrument of the temporal distortion. Seven years of labor became experientially negligible because of love's transformation of experience. This is one of the most celebrated compressed love-statements in world literature — the understatement is the point.
21: hāḇāh ʾet-ʾišĕtî kî mālĕʾû yāmāy wĕʾāḇôʾāh ʾēleyhā — "Give me my wife, for my days are fulfilled, and I will go in to her." Jacob's demand uses the legal formula mālĕʾû yāmāy ("my days are full/completed") — the fulfillment of the contractual term. The verb mālaʾ (H4390, to fill, complete) signals legal completion. ʾĀḇôʾāh ʾēleyhā — "I will come to her" — the idiom for sexual consummation of marriage (cf. Genesis 6:4; Genesis 16:2; Genesis 38:8). Jacob's directness is not rude but legally assertive: the contract is complete; the transfer of the wife should now occur.
22: wayyeʾĕsōp lāḇān ʾet-kol-ʾanĕšê hammāqôm wayyaʿaś mišteh — "Laban gathered all the men of the place and made a feast." The wedding feast (mišteh, H4960, drinking feast, from šātāh, to drink) was the public covenant ratification of marriage in the ANE — analogous to the covenant meals of diplomatic agreements (Genesis 26:30; Genesis 31:54). The communal gathering ("all the men of the place") establishes the public witness that makes the marriage legally valid. The feast is also the cover for Laban's deception: in the evening darkness, after the drinking, the wrong daughter is brought.
23: wayhî ḇāʿereḇ wayyiqqaḥ ʾet-lēʾāh bittô wayyāḇēʾ ʾōtāh ʾēlāyw — "In the evening he took his daughter Leah and brought her to him." The deception operates by darkness (ḇāʿereḇ, in the evening) — the same mechanism Jacob used against his blind father: concealed identity in a dark room. Jacob blinded his father with goat skins and a deceptive voice; Laban blinds Jacob with darkness and a veil. The verb wayyāḇēʾ (he brought) mirrors the language of bringing the bride (v23) but with the wrong subject — it is the father bringing, not the bride coming voluntarily. The irony is structural and explicit: the deceiver is deceived with the same method he used.
24: wayyittēn lāḇān lāh ʾet-zilpāh šipĕḥātô lĕlēʾāh bittô šipĕḥāh — "Laban gave his servant Zilpah to his daughter Leah as her servant." The parenthetical note introduces Zilpah, who will become the mother of Gad and Asher (Genesis 30:9-13). The gift of a servant to a daughter upon marriage was standard ANE custom — the servant formed part of the bride's dowry. Bilhah will similarly be given to Rachel (v29). Both servants-turned-surrogate-mothers are introduced at their respective marriages; their role in the birth-narrative of Genesis 30 is thus legally prepared here.
25: wayhî ḇabbōqer wĕhinnēh-hîʾ lēʾāh wayyōʾmer ʾel-lāḇān mah-zōʾt ʿāśîtā llî — "In the morning — behold, it was Leah!" Jacob's discovery is marked by the same exclamatory hinnēh used at the well-meeting (v2) — but here the surprise is horror, not delight. Lāmmāh rimmîtānî — "why have you deceived me?" — the verb rāmāh (H7411, to throw/betray/deceive) is used here; in 27:35 Jacob is described using mirmāh (deceit, H4820, from the same root) to take Esau's blessing. The verbal link is the narrator's ironic commentary: Jacob, who obtained his father's blessing through deception (mirmāh), is now deceived by the same mechanism (rāmāh).
26: lōʾ-yēʿāśeh ḵēn bimmĕqômēnû lātet haṣṣĕʿîrāh lipnê habbĕḵîrāh — "It is not done this way in our place — to give the younger before the firstborn." Laban's appeal to custom (mišpāṭ implied in yēʿāśeh) as justification turns Jacob's own principle against him: Jacob displaced his older brother; now Laban invokes the elder-before-younger rule to justify the deception. The vocabulary of ṣĕʿîrāh (younger/lesser) and bĕḵîrāh (firstborn/elder) echoes the Esau-Jacob terminology throughout — bĕḵôr (H1060, firstborn) was what Jacob seized from Esau; now Laban uses the bĕḵôr principle to justify depriving Jacob of what he wanted.
27: mallēʾ šĕḇuaʿ zōʾt wĕnittĕnāh lĕḵā gam-ʾet-zōʾt — "Complete this week, and we will give you the other one also." Šāḇûaʿ (H7620, week, period of seven) — the bridal week was the seven-day celebration following a wedding (cf. Judges 14:12 — Samson's seven-day riddle period for the feast). Laban proposes to let Jacob complete the customary bridal week with Leah before receiving Rachel — requiring another seven years of labor in exchange. The double-seven structure (seven years + seven days bridal week + seven more years) saturates the Laban narrative with the "seven" of covenant and completeness, though Laban exploits these patterns for his own gain.
28: wayyaʿas yaʿăqōḇ kēn wayyĕmal šĕḇuaʿ zōʾt wayyittēn-lô ʾet-rāḥēl bittô lô lĕʾiššāh — "Jacob did so; he completed the week with her, and then Laban gave him Rachel his daughter as his wife." The narrative compression — the entire second seven-year period is covered in the first half of v30 — shows that the narrator's interest is in the structural outcome (Jacob's double marriage), not the laboring years. Jacob completes (millēʾ = fulfills, same root as v21's "my days are fulfilled") the bridal week — covenant fidelity even within the exploitative system. His compliance with Laban's terms under duress will generate the covenant grounds for his eventual departure in Genesis 31.
29: wayyittēn lāḇān lĕrāḥēl bittô ʾet-bilhāh šipĕḥātô lāh lĕšipĕḥāh — "Laban gave his servant Bilhah to Rachel his daughter as her servant." Bilhah's introduction parallels Zilpah's in v24 — both servants are gifts from Laban at the marriage of each daughter. The servant-gift is a dowry custom documented in ANE marriage contracts (the Nuzi tablets contain examples of fathers giving slave-girls to daughters upon marriage, with the stipulation that if the daughter is childless, the slave's children will count as the daughter's). This legal background explains Rachel's surrogate-motherhood proposal in Genesis 30:3: she is activating a legally recognized option, not improvising.
30: wayyeʾĕhaḇ gam-ʾet-rāḥēl mimmillēʾāh — "he loved Rachel more than Leah." The comparative construction gam-ʾet-rāḥēl mimmillēʾāh — literally "also Rachel more than Leah" — with min (from/more than) expressing comparison. The double love is stated (Jacob loved both), but the comparative love for Rachel is the narrative tension that drives Genesis 30. Wayyaʿăḇōḏ ʿimmô ʾôḏ šeḇaʿ-šānîm ʾăḥērôt — "he worked for him another seven years" — the second labor period is dispatched in a single clause. The fourteen-year total is implicit in the structure; Jacob's love is measured in labor.
31: wayyarʾ YHWH kî-śĕnûʾāh lēʾāh wayyipteaḥ ʾet-raḥmāh wĕrāḥēl ʿăqārāh — "When YHWH saw that Leah was hated/unloved, he opened her womb; but Rachel was barren." Śĕnûʾāh (Qal passive participle of śānēʾ, to hate) — the Hebrew term "hated" functions as a comparative idiom in biblical law and narrative: Deuteronomy 21:15-17 discusses the rights of the son of the "hated" wife (śĕnûʾāh), meaning the less-favored wife. The "hatred" is comparative, not absolute — Leah is loved less, not despised. Wayyipteaḥ ʾet-raḥmāh — "he opened her womb" — peṯaḥ (to open) applied to reḥem (H7358, womb). Notably, reḥem shares the root with raḥămîm (H7356, compassion, mercy) — God opens the womb in an act of compassionate attention to the unloved woman. YHWH acts where the husband does not love.
32: wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô rĕʾûḇen kî ʾāmĕrāh kî-rāʾāh YHWH bĕʿonyî kî ʿattāh yeʾĕhāḇanî ʾîšî — "She called his name Reuben, for she said, 'YHWH has seen my affliction; surely now my husband will love me.'" The name rĕʾûḇen encodes a wordplay: it sounds like rāʾāh-ben ("see, a son!") and also encodes rāʾāh bĕʿonyî ("YHWH has seen my affliction"). ʿOnî (H6040, affliction, humiliation, misery — from ʿānāh, to be bowed down/afflicted) is the word used for Israel's oppression in Egypt (Exodus 3:7, 17: "I have seen the affliction of my people"). Leah's personal affliction anticipates the national affliction of her descendants. The hope "now my husband will love me" (yeʾĕhāḇanî ʾîšî) will be disappointed — the pattern of divine compassion compensating for human lovelessness continues through all four name-sayings in this chapter.
33: wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô šimʿôn kî-šāmaʿ YHWH kî-śĕnûʾāh ʾānōḵî wayyittēn-lî gam-ʾet-zeh — "She named him Simeon, for YHWH has heard that I am unloved, and he gave me this one also." Šimʿôn = "one who hears" — from šāmaʿ (H8085, to hear, to obey). The divine hearing is the covenant mechanism: YHWH hears the cry of the afflicted (Exodus 3:7; Psalms 22:24; Genesis 34:17). Leah's naming of her sons is a running theological commentary: YHWH sees (Reuben), YHWH hears (Simeon), YHWH will join (Levi), I will praise YHWH (Judah). The escalating intimacy of the names — from observed affliction to spontaneous praise — traces Leah's theological journey through the birth of four sons.
34: wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô lēwî kî ʾāmĕrāh ʿattāh yillāweh ʾîšî ʾēlay — "She named him Levi, for she said, 'Now my husband will be joined to me.'" Lēwî from lāwāh (H3867, to join, to accompany, to attach) — the name expresses Leah's hope for conjugal attachment. The Levites' name will carry this meaning forward: in Numbers 18:2-4, the Levites are "joined" (yillāwû) to Aaron in priestly service — the name of the tribe that becomes the priestly caste encodes the longing of the unbeloved mother to be joined to her husband. The irony deepens: Leah wanted to be joined to Jacob; her descendants will be joined to YHWH in the priesthood.
35: wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô yĕhûḏāh kî ʾāmĕrāh happaʿam ʾôḏeh ʾet-YHWH — "She named him Judah, for she said, 'This time I will praise YHWH.'" Yĕhûḏāh from yādāh (H3034, to throw/praise/give thanks, Hiphil: to praise) — the name means "praise/thanksgiving." In the arc of Leah's four birth-sayings, Judah's naming is the climax: she no longer asks for Jacob's love or God's intervention; she simply praises. The theological movement is from petition (see my affliction) through thanksgiving (God heard) through hope (will be joined) to pure doxology (I will praise). This shift — from petition to praise — is the pattern of the Psalms of lament and the shape of covenant prayer. Waṯṯaʿămōḏ milddet — "and she stopped bearing" — the narrative pause prepares for the crisis of Genesis 30.
